title: Transportation Technology
description: RAND's research on transportation technology explores how innovations such as autonomous vehicles, electric mobility, and intelligent infrastructure can improve safety, efficiency, and sustainability. Studies assess regulatory frameworks, societal impacts, and strategies for integrating emerging technologies into existing transportation systems.
text:
How Artificial Intelligence Could Reshape Four Essential Competitions in Future Warfare
The authors offer a conceptual framework and preliminary assessment of how artificial intelligence could affect four “building block” competitions in military affairs.
Learning and remembering under fire: Proposals for a novel doctrinal system for Ukraine
This report examines doctrine and technology for Ukraine. It examines the role of doctrine for militaries and how other countries use doctrine, and ends with recommendations for action to create a doctrinal system suited for Ukraine's purposes.
Artificial Intelligence Adoption and Sectoral Transformation: Implications for Health Care, Financial Services, Climate and Energy, and Transportation
Advancing artificial intelligence (AI) capabilities will have profound legal, policy, and business implications. In this report, the authors present and use the AI Capabilities Framework to examine AI’s transformative potential in key sectors.
The People's Liberation Army's Approach to Manned-Unmanned Teaming: Theory and Practice
This analysis of publicly available information on Chinese perspectives of manned-unmanned teaming (MUM-T) in air combat can inform U.S. Department of the Air Force leaders and strategists as they develop advanced U.S. MUM-T warfighting concepts.
United States Navy Force Structure: The Challenge of Global Crisis Response
The author addresses the mismatch between the challenges the U.S. Navy will likely face in the international arena and its force structure and considers potential force structure shortfalls and possible adjustments to force structure development.
Air Taxis Are Coming Soon to a City Near You. What Does That Mean for the Communities Below Them?
Electric air taxis are coming soon to a city near you. But city skies are already crowded. Whether adding air taxis to the mix will benefit more than wealthy jet-setters will depend heavily on whether the communities below help determine where and how they fly.
A Swarm at Sea: Supplying Troops with On-Demand Autonomous Watercraft
Using low-cost, rapidly built, small autonomous surface vessels to deliver supplies would be a less vulnerable and more scalable method than relying on large, mostly unarmed, civilian-crewed ships. This approach could provide advanced logistics that are difficult for U.S. adversaries to counter.
The US Navy's Uncrewed Future
The emergence of increasingly capable uncrewed technologies may help make the navies of the mid-21st century more effective and risk-tolerant, enabling them to secure the peace for decades to come.
Small Uncrewed Aircraft Systems (SUAS) in Divisional Brigades: Electronic Warfare and Spectrum Management Considerations
The authors assessed challenges and opportunities related to integrating small uncrewed aircraft systems with electronic warfare capabilities in divisional brigades and identified ways to make XVIII Airborne Corps units more effective in meeting spectrum management challenges.
Small Uncrewed Aircraft Systems (SUAS) in Divisional Brigades: Small UAS and Counter-UAS Training
The authors identified options for the Army to field and employ small uncrewed aircraft systems at the division, BCT and battalion levels to enhance fire support capabilities.
Small Uncrewed Aircraft Systems (SUAS) in Divisional Brigades: Survey of SUAS
The authors identified options for the Army to field, and employ small, uncrewed aircraft systems (SUAS) including commercial off-the-shelf systems, the Defense Innovation Unit's Blue Uncrewed Aircraft Systems list, and the commercial market overall.
Small Uncrewed Aircraft Systems in Divisional Brigades: Requirements and Findings
In this overview volume of a five-part series, the authors present their findings on how the U.S. Army can improve its training, acquisition, and use of small uncrewed aircraft systems to better prepare for combat operations.
